idea summary:
Archive of Our Own, or Ao3, is an online archive of fanfiction (i.e. stories or other kinds of art about / set in a preexisting intellectual property, written by fans for free, purely for the love of the game) and other transformative works. Fanfiction as a writing genre, particularly as stored on Ao3, differs from traditional fiction in that stories are heavily tagged with metadata that flags information about the story up front, including certain types of character relationships, content warnings, content ratings, among other things.
My product idea is to develop a Python-based program that scrapes the metadata for a particular fandom on Ao3 (read: compiles a table of metadata for all the works listed under a particular intellectual property on Ao3), then builds a data dashboard that summarizes the fandom’s collective metadata in a dashboard.
information source:
Archive of Our Own web pages / web scraping 
(Example: https://archiveofourown.org/works/71539056/chapters/186230116)
insight enablement:
Because Ao3 fics are so thoroughly subject-matter tagged, Ao3 metadata could be a fruitful source of information for digital humanities studies focused on fandom literature – with fanfiction as a proxy for cultural conversations about any given intellectual property with a robust presence on Archive of Our Own.
My ideal end users would be interested fans and fandom scholars, who could apply my product to their Ao3 fandom of interest.
msim specialization overlap areas: This project would involve data-focused web scraping, dashboard development, and data analysis, which overlaps neatly with my data science studies. The project would also be informed by my UX studies in that I would seek feedback from a sample of intended users on what metrics they would most be interested in seeing on such a dashboard.

idea summary: My product idea is to develop a program that identifies and maps ADA-accessible hiking trails in WA State for disabled hikers, who are interested in planning day or overnight trips to Washington state and national parks. While there are fairly comprehensive databases of Washington state hiking trails, there are not necessarily comprehensive databases that are tailored for the purpose of identifying accessible outdoor spaces for disabled WA state residents, beyond just including fields that state whether a trail is accessible or not.
information source: WA State Recreation and Conservation Office trails database: https://trails-wa-rco.hub.arcgis.com/search?tags=Category%2Chiking%2520trails%2Ctrail%2Ctrails%2Cbiking%2520trails
insight enablement: Everyone deserves to be able to enjoy the great outdoors. A tool that can pull from centralized state recreation databases and produce an interactive map of accessible hiking trails could be a useful platform / jumping-off point for mapping physical accessibility in other kinds of spaces (e.g. university buildings, classrooms, government buildings, local parks, coffee shops, museums, Renaissance Faires, etc.).
msim specialization overlap areas: This project would involve data cleaning and analysis and dashboard development, which overlaps with my data science studies. It would also be informed by my UX studies in that I would seek feedback from disabled hikers / outdoorspeople on their wants and needs regarding trail information.

idea summary: My product idea is to develop a tool that compiles and presents data about disability accommodations (i.e. available accommodations, accommodation application process, disability rights track record, etc.) at different universities for disabled junior and senior-year high school students, researching where to apply to college.
information source: This idea is less built out than the other two, but sources I’m considering / researching to identify data sources include university disability accommodations web pages, the National Center for Education Statistics (https://nces.ed.gov/), and the ADA national network (https://adata.org/research_brief/higher-education-and-ada). My goal is to be able to include information both on what accommodations these universities offer / describe on their webpages, and information from outside sources indicating how effectively they implement their accommodations.
insight enablement: In my experience as a student with pretty comprehensive DRS accommodations, there can be a significant gap between “on-paper” disability accommodations and the actual experience of applying for, securing, and implementing those accommodations. There are certain things I wish I’d known before starting the MSIM program about the time and energy I would need to put in to make sure my accommodations were best supporting me as a student. I would love to see a tool that helps other disabled prospective students evaluate what their experience might be like at any given university where they are submitting an application.
msim specialization overlap areas: My sense is that this project would end up looking like a data dashboard, which would pull in my data science expertise, but would be more heavily UX-focused, since the end product would be reporting to satisfy a broad range of user needs.
